coredata.dc:title: Mass production of tunable multicolor graphene quantum dots from an energy resource of coke by a one-step electrochemical exfoliation
coredata.dc:creator: He, Meiqin
Guo, Xinrong
Huang, Jianzhi
Shen, Huanhuan
Zeng, Qiang
Wang, Lishi
coredata.dc:description: Herein, we originally propose a simple and facile one-step electrochemical exfoliation approach to synthesize in large scale graphene quantum dots (GQDs) from low-cost energy resource of coke. We found that the GQDs emerged multi-color fluorescence by adjusting the water content of electrolyte solution and applying current density, which effectually caused green-GQDs (G-GQDs), yellow-GQDs (Y-GQDs), and orange-GQDs (O-GQDs) of regular micro-variation in size from 3.02 to 4.61 nm with the fluorescence emissions at 500, 530 and 560 nm, respectively. Furthermore, blue-GQDs (B-GQDs) with emission at 440 nm can be obtained by reducing G-GQDs with NaBH4 at room temperature for 4 h. The quantum yields of B-, G-, Y- and O-GQDs are obtained up to 19.27%, 8.47%, 7.90% and 9.24%, respectively. The B-, G-, Y- and O-GQDs with high yields severally corresponding to 13.04 wt%, 17.88 wt%, 42.86 wt% and 31.13 wt%, possess scalable and industrial production prospect. All four types of GQDs were fully characterized and the electrochemical production mechanism of these GQDs has been also investigated in full. Additionally, GQDs were also available to fabricate their solid-state GQDs/epoxy composites for potential applications in multicolor light-emitting diode devices.

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: The chemical reduction mechanism by NaBH4

xocs:funding.xocs:funding-agency-matched-string: National Natural Science Foundation of China
xocs:funding.xocs:funding-agency: National Natural Science Foundation of China

xocs:funding.xocs:funding-agency-matched-string: Fundamental Research Funds for the Central Universities
xocs:funding.xocs:funding-agency: Fundamental Research Funds for the Central Universities

xocs:funding-list.xocs:funding-text: This work was financially supported by the National Natural Science Foundation of China (Grant Nos. 21475046 , 21427809 and 21645004 ) and the Fundamental Research Funds for the Central Universities (Grant Nos. 2015ZP028 and 2017MS094 ).

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Schematic illustration of the synthetic process of multicolor GQDs from coke. (A colour version of this figure can be viewed online.)

The SEM images (A, B) are of coke powers; The TEM images are of O- (C), Y- (D), G- (E) and B-GQDs (F); The HRTEM images are of O- (G), Y- (H), G- (I) and B-GQDs (J). (A colour version of this figure can be viewed online.)
AFM images are corresponding (A) O-GQDs, (B) Y-GQDs, (C) G- and (D) B-GQDs. (A colour version of this figure can be viewed online.)

UV–Vis absorption spectra of GQDs (left); The embedded photographs of GQDs under UV lamp with 365 nm excitation wave (middle); Fluorescence emission spectra under different excitation waves (right) are corresponding to O- (A), Y- (B), G- (C) and B-GQDs (D), respectively; (E) The color of G-GQDs solutions changed over time for 0 h, 2 h and 4 h; (F) The maximum emission of G-GQDs shows blue-shift over time. (A colour version of this figure can be viewed online.)
The FT-IR spectra of the GQDs (A) and coke (B); The Raman spectra of the GQDs (C) and coke (D); The XRD spectra of the GQDs (E) and coke (F). (A colour version of this figure can be viewed online.)
The XPS spectra of C1s of O- (A), Y- (B), G- (C) and B-GQDs (D). (A colour version of this figure can be viewed online.)
The time-resolved PL decay profiles are of O- (A), Y- (B), G- (C) and B-GQDs (D). (A colour version of this figure can be viewed online.)

Photo (A) of the exfoliated process showing the attack of the coke edge planes by •OH and •O radicals, which benefits the insertion of SO4 •−; (B) The anodic oxidation of H2O (step 1) and intercalation of SO4 •− (step 2) cause together the formation of the stripped products. (A colour version of this figure can be viewed online.)

(A) The maximum emission peaks and colors of GQDs at different H2O contents and current densities; The as-prepared GQDs were illuminated under daylight lamp (B) and UV light at 365 nm excitation wave (C). (A colour version of this figure can be viewed online.)
Schematic is of the mechanism for B-GQDs via G-GQDs by NaBH4 reduction. (A colour version of this figure can be viewed online.)
The fitting of fluorescence decay data of O-, Y-, G- and B-GQDs.

head.ce:title: Mass production of tunable multicolor graphene quantum dots from an energy resource of coke by a one-step electrochemical exfoliation

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: School of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering, South China University of Technology, Guangzhou, 510641, People's Republic of China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: School of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Herein, we originally propose a simple and facile one-step electrochemical exfoliation approach to synthesize in large scale graphene quantum dots (GQDs) from low-cost energy resource of coke. We found that the GQDs emerged multi-color fluorescence by adjusting the water content of electrolyte solution and applying current density, which effectually caused green-GQDs (G-GQDs), yellow-GQDs (Y-GQDs), and orange-GQDs (O-GQDs) of regular micro-variation in size from 3.02 to 4.61 nm with the fluorescence emissions at 500, 530 and 560 nm, respectively. Furthermore, blue-GQDs (B-GQDs) with emission at 440 nm can be obtained by reducing G-GQDs with NaBH4 at room temperature for 4 h. The quantum yields of B-, G-, Y- and O-GQDs are obtained up to 19.27%, 8.47%, 7.90% and 9.24%, respectively. The B-, G-, Y- and O-GQDs with high yields severally corresponding to 13.04 wt%, 17.88 wt%, 42.86 wt% and 31.13 wt%, possess scalable and industrial production prospect. All four types of GQDs were fully characterized and the electrochemical production mechanism of these GQDs has been also investigated in full. Additionally, GQDs were also available to fabricate their solid-state GQDs/epoxy composites for potential applications in multicolor light-emitting diode devices.

ce:section.ce:para: Graphene quantum dots (GQDs), which were first prepared from graphene sheets via hydrothermal method in 2010 [1], consist of a few layered graphitic crystalline structures. In comparison with quasi-spherical carbon quantum dots (CQDs) [2], GQDs possess larger specific surface area, abundant edge sites, higher crystallinity and smaller size [2,3]. These promising properties make GQDs more attractive for many comprehensive applications, for examples in bioimaging [4,5], biosensing [6,7], electronic displays [8], drug/gene delivery [9,10], anticancer agents [11,12] and photocatalysis [13]. At present, carbon precursors of GQDs prevailingly cover graphene [1,14], graphite [15], carbon nanotube [16–18], carbon fiber [19,20], active carbon [21,22] and their likes. But these carbonaceous materials are relatively high-cost (Table S1), and some are required with high purity. Consequently, there is significant practical value to explore an alternative low-cost carbon material to prepare GQDs.

Coal, an earth-abundant and inexpensive (Table S2) natural fossil fuel [23,24] praised as “black gold” [25,26] and “food for industry” [27], is principally used as an energy resource for burning [28,29]. Coal includes nano-sized graphitic crystalline carbon areas, which are linked by disordered amorphous aliphatic side chains [30,31]. Thereby, it is possible to produce carbon based quantum dots (QDs) from coal by destroying these weak aliphatic side chains. In 2013, coal firstly applied as natural and economy carbon source to synthesize GQDs by oxidation with concentrated H2SO4 and HNO3 (3:1) at 120 °C for 24 h [32]. Hereafter, the chemical oxidation of coal with strong acid was utilized and improved in many analogous reports to obtain GQDs or CQDs [33–36]. For examples, blue N-doped CQDs were successfully synthesized by Wu et al. from petroleum coke by oxidizing with concentrated acid and functionalizing with hydrothermal ammonia treatment [37]. Li et al. [38] also obtained the blue CQDs with a high quantum yield (QY) up to 18.6% from anthracite coal by HNO3 oxidation and grafting organic amino molecules. But these discussed cases all require long treatment time (>24 h), harsh reaction conditions and tedious pre-oxidation steps. As a consequence, the hydrothermal method was employed by Sasikala to produce blue GQDs from anthracite coal by mild oxidizing supercritical water only in 2 h [39]. By contrast, it provides a more convenient and green approach to manufacture GQDs. Actually, the vast majority of the reported GQDs or CQDs merely emit single fluorescence ranging from blue to green color [40]. Therefore, it is a great challenge to develop a convenient and efficient method for producing multi-color GQDs from cheap coal with the crucial need particularly in applications in multi-color patterning, ratiometric technique and multidimensional sense [41].

To address the afore-discussed issues, electrochemical exfoliation method with the merits of convenience, low cost, high efficiency and mass outputs, offers a promising strategy for the preparation and application of carbon based QDs. Unfortunately, almost all of natural coal possesses poor conductivity due to the isolated nanometer-sized crystalline clusters in the structure [42–45]. Recently, investigation on coal annealing reveals that annealing enables to increase C sp
2 content, localization length and density of edge-plane-like defective sites on coal, which make a significant contribution to its electrical properties [46]. Coke is usually made from raw coal by heating at 1000 °C with high carbon content and few impurities [47]. These properties make coke as a good candidate of starting carbonaceous material for generation of GQDs [37]. Additionally, the thermal treatment for coke-forming leads to a direct improvement of C sp
2 content and expansion of aromatic domain size in coal and it is able to enlarge the electrical conductivity of coke over 7 orders of magnitude [48,49]. Thus, it's highly possible to produce GQDs from coke by the simple but efficient electrochemical method.

In this work, we originally developed a convenient one-step strategy basing on electrochemical stripping for the synthesis of GQDs from low-cost coke and investigated their potential production mechanism, where the cut piece of commercial coke is directly used as the working electrode (Fig. 1 ). Interestingly, by regulating the current density applied to coke and water (H2O) content of electrolyte solution, emission positions of GQDs can be precisely tailored, resulting in multi-color fluorescence in green (G-GQDs), yellow (Y-GQDs) and orange (O-GQDs). More importantly, an interesting two-step electrochemical production mechanism of GQDs has been investigated in full. Moreover, the as-prepared G-GQDs can be further reduced by NaBH4 to form new GQDs, emitting bright blue fluorescence (B-GQDs). The B-, G-, Y- and O-GQDs with high yields up to 13.04 wt%, 17.88 wt%, 42.86 wt% and 31.13 wt%, own a great mass-produced feature.

Coke, provided by Benxi Coal Mine, Liaoning, China, was cut to small cuboid pieces (0.5 cm × 0.5 cm × 2 cm). (NH4)2S2O8 and NaBH4 were received from J&K Scientific Ltd (China). Methanol (MeOH), quinine sulfate and Rhodamine B were purchased from Shanghai Aladdin Bio-Chem Technology Co., LTD (China). The water used in the experiment was the double distilled water. Aforementioned chemical reagents were analytical grade and used without further purification. Epoxy and epoxy hardener were bought from Hangzhou Wuhui Port Adhesive Co., Ltd (China).

Electricity was provided with self-ordained digital controlled direct current stabilized voltage source. The micro-profile parameters were studied via a field emission scanning electron microscope (SEM) (FE-SEM; Zeiss Ultra55, Germany). SEM samples were prepared by milling coke particles into powders at 600 rpm in a QM-3SP4 ball-mill for 48 h. The transmission electron microscopy (TEM) images were characterized on a JEOL-2100F microscope. The Atomic Force Microscope (AFM) characterizations were recorded on a Multimode Nano-scope V scanning probe microscopy system. Fluorescence spectra and the time-resolved emission spectra (TRES) were carried out by a PerkinElmer LS-55 fluorescence spectrometer. Raman spectra were recorded on a LabRAM Aramis spectrometer with a He-Ne (532 nm) laser excitation source. UV–Vis absorption spectra were recorded on a Perkin Elmer UV–Visible-Lambda 25 spectrophotometer. Fourier transform infrared spectroscopy (FT-IR) was performed with a Nicolet 6700 spectrometer. X-ray diffraction (XRD) patterns were measured on a Bruker D4 X-ray diffractometer. X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) measurements were detected on ESCALAB 250 Xi. The binding energies were corrected to avoid charging effect with referencing C1s position of 284.8 eV.

The electrochemical experiment was conducted in a two-electrode system, where using a platinum plate electrode as the counter electrode and a small cuboid coke lump as the working electrode. The coke lump was successively treated with ultrasound in water to remove the dust, sand [50] and in isopropanol to get rid of the Al, Si [46,51]. The electrolysis of coke was carried out in the mixture of MeOH and H2O at different volume ratios varying from 10:90 to 85:15 with 0.1 mol L−1 of (NH4)2S2O8 (pH = 7.0) as an electrolyte for 1 h, where current intensities ranging from 80 to 400 mA cm−2 applied. Be aware of the saturation of (NH4)2S2O8 in the mixture, the volume ratio of MeOH to H2O should be kept lower than 6. The GQDs were centrifuged at 12000 rpm for 15 min to remove the large exfoliated coke particles. The supernatant of above GQDs was purified by a dialysis bag with intercepted molecular weight of 1000 Da for 3 days. To further investigate the structures and properties of the GQDs, representative GQDs are selected as model examples. In particular, the typical GQDs were respectively synthesized under below stated conditions: MeOH: H2O = 85:15 at 40 mA cm−2 for O-GQDs, MeOH: H2O = 60:40 at 120 mA cm−2 for Y-GQDs and MeOH: H2O = 30:70 at 240 mA cm−2 for G-GQDs. The B-GQDs were prepared by chemical reduction method. 1 mg/ml NaBH4 was added to the 30 mg/ml above 50 ml G-GQDs solution and then the mixture solution was vigorously stirred at room temperature for 4 h. The supernatant was dialyzed with 1000 Da cutoff membrane for 3 days, finally acquiring bright B-GQDs solution.

In order to synthesize GQDs/epoxy composite, 0.8 ml epoxy hardener and 2 ml epoxy was in order added to 0.5 mL high concentrated O-GQDs solution (in dichloromethane) and disposed by ultrasound to get a homogeneous solution. Then the mixture was transferred into a 10 ml clean glass beaker and dried to form a transparent composite. The Y-, G- and B-GQDs/epoxy composites all were analogously prepared.

The yield of GQDs was calculated by the following methods [52]. Firstly the GQDs aqueous solution was dried to solid powder and weighed. The yield of O-GQDs was equivalent to the weight ratios of the solid GQDs and the exfoliated coke particles. Similarly, the yields of G-GQDs and Y-GQDs were figured. While the yield of B-GQDs was equal to the multiplication of the G-GQDs yield and the conversion rate of the G-GQDs to B-GQDs.

The QYs of the GQDs were all calculated by doing comparison as the following equation (Eq) (1):
(1)
Q Y = Q
Y
r e f
η
2
η
r e f
2
I
I
r e f
A
r e f
A
where QY
ref represents the QY of the standard, η and η
ref correspond to the refractive index of the GQDs and standard, I and I
ref belong to the integral areas of specimen and reference fluorescence spectra, and A and A
ref coincide with the UV absorption of measured samples and standard substance. Additionally, the QYs of the B- and G-GQDs were counted by applying quinine sulfate (QY = 54.6%) in 0.1 M H2SO4 (η = 1.33) as the standard [53–56]. While the QYs of Y- and O-GQDs were figured by employing Rhodamine B (QY = 31.0%) in water (η = 1.33) as reference [57–59]. The emission spectrum of reference should match that of the specimens as closely as possible [60]. The UV absorptions at the excitation wavelength were less than 0.05 with the purpose of avoiding internal filter effects [60].

During the electrochemical exfoliation of coke, as shown in the selected example of Y-GQDs (Fig. S1), the electrolyte color gradually changed from colorless to light yellow at the initial exfoliation stage and then the coke anode took place a slight expansion, making the electrolyte color even darker. Finally, the distensible flakes and particles dropped down from the coke anodes to form the dark yellow slurry. These three obvious phenomena can be described as the different dynamic electrolytic progresses corresponding to electrochemical oxidation, intercalation and expansion, respectively [15]. During this decomposition course, it can be clearly observed a large number of bubbles generating at anode, which were caused by O2 (generated from water decomposition). The yields of the O-, Y-, G- and B-GQDs were severally obtained as high as 31.13 wt%, 42.86 wt%, 17.88 wt% and 13.04 wt%. The relative QYs of O-, Y-, G- and B-GQDs respectively reached up to 9.24%, 7.90%, 8.47% and 19.27% (Tables S3–S4), which are higher than those prepared by the hydrothermal cleaving method (<8%) [53,61,62]. Our work is also compared with the analogous synthesis for coal-derived CDs or GQDs (Table S5), certifying that the electrochemical exfoliation is efficient to synthesize high-quality GQDs from the common energy resource of coke.

Additionally, it has been reported that GQDs can be also produced from some alcohols [63,64]. In order to clarify this issue, a control experiment was conducted to prove that coke acts as the unique carbon source in this work for GQDs rather than MeOH from the component of electrolyte solution. Fortunately, it is positive that there is no detectable fluorescence from the electrolyte solutions after electrolysis (Fig. S2).

SEMs manifest that microscopic coke has an irregular size and shape distribution (Fig. 2 A) and contains flake-like structures (Fig. 2B), which dramatically increase the specific surface areas for adequately touching to the electrolyte solution. As described by EDS, coke is composed of 5 main elements including 84.41 wt% of C; 11.74 wt% of O, 1.46 wt% of Al, 1.12 wt% of Si and 1.26 wt% of S (Fig. S3). The high carbon content indicates that coke is a potential natural carbonaceous material. As illustrated in the TEM images (Fig. 2C–F), the average sizes of O-, Y-, G- and B-GQDs were 4.61 ± 0.88, 4.15 ± 0.54, 3.02 ± 0.45, 2.90 ± 0.46 nm, respectively. The HRTEM pictures (Fig. 2G–J) exhibit that the obvious lattice spacing of O-, Y-, G- and B-GQDs is severally 0.35, 0.34, 0.33 and 0.33 nm, which correspond to the (002) diffraction facets of graphite planes. As discussed above, the TEM and HRTEM studies imply that the as-produced GQDs have high crystallinity.

The topographic morphology and height profile of GQDs were investigated by AFM. As shown in Fig. 3 , the average heights of the O-, Y-, G- and B-GQDs are respectively 2.4, 2.0, 1.6 and 1.3 nm, indicating that the most O- and Y-GQDs consist of three to four layers of graphene similar structures, and G- and B-GQDs are composed of bi-to triple-layered ones [65]. In addition, the average size and height of B-GQDs are similar with the G-GQDs, which suggest that the fluorescence blue shift of B-GQDs could due to their superficial structural changes after reduction by NaBH4 rather than the size variations. This result is consistent with previous reports [65,66]. The large gaps between diameter and height distribution of the GQDs convincingly prove that we get plate-like GQDs rather than quasi-spherical CQDs.

As shown in Fig. 4 A–D, all GQDs exhibit a strong UV absorption located between 200 and 230 nm typically due to the π-π∗ electron transition of CC bonds [66,67], and a shoulder centered between 300 and 350 nm possibly ascribe to the n-π∗ transformation of CO bonds [68–70]. This phenomenon is in accordance with GQDs from graphite rod synthesized by electrochemical method [71]. Maximum emission peaks are observed at 560, 530, 500 and 440 nm when the O-, Y-, G- and B-GQDs aqueous solutions are stimulated at 470, 450, 380 and 340 nm, respectively. On the basis of the wide size gap among O-, Y- and G-GQDs (Fig. 2C–F), the red shift of the emission wavelength associating with the incremental size of GQDs may be partly attributed to quantum confinement effect [19], and more in-depth studies on the reasons of red shift are subsequently executed. In addition, it's obvious that all GQDs undergo a red shift on the fluorescence emission as the excitation wavelength increases, namely excitation-dependent behavior [57,72,73]. This behavior is common for most of CQDs and GQDs and is due to the difference in the GQDs surface states for excitons capture [5,74,75]. To monitor the reduction assignment of G-GQDs with NaBH4, the changes of G-GQDs in fluorescence have been recorded. As exhibited in Fig. 4E, the addition of NaBH4 to G-GQDs resulted in the fluorescent color changing from light green to bright blue with an inter-media color of dark green. The maximum emissions of the GQDs were respectively at 500, 460 and 440 nm when adding NaBH4 for 0, 2 and 4 h (Fig. 4F). Obviously, the emission wavelength of G-GQDs carried out blue shift with the increasing reaction time reduced by NaBH4. Furthermore, the GQDs aqueous solutions with high fluorescence stability are still exhibit strong fluorescence after stored in a refrigerator at 4 °C for 8 months. The multi-color GQDs aqueous solutions were also solidified to transform into transparent solid-state GQDs/epoxy composites by adding epoxy and epoxy hardener (Fig. S4A) emitting corresponding fluorescence under UV light (Fig. S4B). The maximum emission of composite basically kept consistent with the GQDs solution (Fig. S4C), which implied that the GQDs/epoxy composite is promising candidate for the encapsulation technology of LED chips.

FT-IR spectra of GQDs (Fig. 5 A) and coke (Fig. 5B) display four characteristic peaks. The typical broad peaks centered at about 3411 cm−1 and 3326 cm−1 are both due to stretch of OH groups [76]. The apparent absorption peaks located at around 1729 cm−1 and 1723 cm−1 are resulted from stretching of CO groups. The obvious peaks existence of 1623 cm−1 and 1568 cm−1 correspond to of CC. Additionally, the peaks at 1135 cm−1 and 1090 cm−1 are in connection with the characteristic peaks of CO groups. Making comparisons, it's clearly observed that the contents of OH, CO and CC groups in GQDs are obviously higher than those in coke, proving that we have successfully synthesized the GQDs. The ratio of CO (peak 1 and 3 circled in red) to OH (peak 2 and 4 circled in red) cut from 1.43 in G-GQDs down to 0.92 in B-GQDs, along with the incremental content of CO groups, which certified that partial CO groups were reduced to OH groups after treatment by NaBH4 [77,78].

By Raman spectroscopy, the characteristic peaks at 1358 cm−1 (Figs. 5C) and 1352 cm−1 (Fig. 5D) both belong to the D-band, which represents the disorder degree of carbon materials. And the typical peaks at 1598 cm−1 and 1606 cm−1 correspond to the G-band. Particularly, the G-band attributes to the vibration of the sp
2 carbon atoms in the two-dimensional hexagonal lattice, which depends on the graphitization extents of carbonaceous materials. The results demonstrate that the coke-based GQDs principally constitute of sp
2 graphitic carbon and sp
3 amorphous carbon. The typical peaks labeled at 2687 and 2922 cm−1 correspond to 2D and 2G bands. They are the represent of few layers graphene structure, which further proves that coke owns graphite-like structures [31]. The result agrees with the above-mentioned SEM images. As is known to all, the ratio of I
D
/I
G symbolizes the graphitized degree, and the lower value suggests the larger content of the sp
2 carbon cluster [79]. The calculated I
D
/I
G ratios of B-, G-, Y- and O-GQDs are in the order of 0.91, 0.87, 0.83 and 0.78. The ever-decreasing values from B-, G-, Y- to O-GQDs imply the ever-increasing graphite content, which is consistent with previous reports [80].

XRD of GQDs (Fig. 5E) and coke (Fig. 5F) display (002) peaks at around 26° corresponding to interlayer spacing of about 0.34 nm, which is close proximity to graphite (≈0.35 nm) [79]. It powerfully demonstrates that the core areas of GQDs are principally composed of highly conjugate sp
2 carbon domains in the form of close packing. The GQDs possess the smaller interlayer spacing (ca. 0.35 nm) than coke (ca. 0.38 nm) at around 26°, which attributes to the functional groups or sp
3 C on the edge of GQDs broaden the interlayer distance of (002) band [79]. Moreover, coke (Fig. 5F) manifests the graphite (100) band in the neighborhood of 44°, which derives from the small graphite-like structure in coal [30].

To study the chemical compositions and valance states of GQDs, XPS spectra were used to characterize the surface functional groups of the GQDs. The as-prepared GQDs mainly consist of C and O elements (Fig. S5) and are all fitted into three types of C valance states: sp
2 C (CC), sp
3 C(CO) and CO groups (Fig. 6 ), which are approximately situated at 284.8, 286.2 and 288.6eV, severally [52]. The C content gradually increases with the fluorescent red shift (Table S6), which demonstrates that O-GQDs have a higher degree of carbonization than B-, G- and Y-GQDs. This result is in accordance with the conclusions of Raman study. The proportion of CO to CO reduces from 0.83 in G-GQDs down to 0.68 in B-GQDs, which proves that some carbonyl groups were reduced to hydroxyl groups [77,78]. The XPS analysis conclusion is in line with FT-IR spectrum one.

For purpose of further exploring the photoluminescence (PL) origin of GQDs, the time-resolved emission spectra (TRES) of O-, Y-, G- and B-GQDs were respectively executed at 560, 530, 500 and 440 nm emission wavelengths at room temperature (Fig. 7 A–D). The double exponential PL decay curves of GQDs were well fitted according to Eq. (2)
(2)
I
(
t
)
=
A
1
exp
(
− t /
τ
1
)
+
A
2
exp
(
− t /
τ
2
)
where I(t) represents intensity, τ1 and τ2 correspond to the decay time constants, A1 and A2 belong to the weight coefficients of τ1 and τ2, respectively [81,82]. In addition, the average lifetime (τavg) of as-prepared GQDs was figured by using the following Eq. (3):
(3)
τ
a v g
=
(
A
1
τ
1
2
+
A
2
τ
2
2
)
/
(
A
1
τ
1
+
A
2
τ
2
)

The bi-exponential behavior of decay time can be divided into two emissive sites, which are the intrinsic state (fast decay components) and defect state (slow decay components) [78,83]. Compared with intrinsic state (sp 2 C nano domains inserted in sp 3 C matrix compose of high-crystalline carbogenic core), the emission deriving from defect state with oxygenous functional groups (surface energy traps) generally manifests a longer lifetime [78,83–85].

As listed in Table 1 , the short lifetime (τ1) of 1.95–3.94 ns may be due to intrinsic state emission, while the long lifetime (τ2) of 4.30–10.73 ns may be ascribed to defect state emission, which proves that the lifetime of the synthesized GQDs is derived from the intrinsic state emission as well as defect state emission. Additionally, the weight coefficients of τ2 represented slow decay components in G-GQDs (55.25%) is higher than that in B-GQDs (45.04%), whereas the weight coefficients of τ1 symbolized fast decay components in G-GQDs (44.75%) is lower than that in B-GQDs (54.96%). The enormous transformation can be attributed to the reduction of NaBH4. After introducing suitable NaBH4, the superficial carbonyl and carboxyl functional groups of G-GQDs are drastically eliminated, which directly leads to the obvious reduction of surface states on G-GQDs. Consequently, the dominant PL emission converts from surface state emission with emitting green fluorescence into the intrinsic state emission with emitting blue fluorescence, which is in accordance with the reports [78,84,87]. Moreover, the PL decay time of defect state emission (560–440 nm) gradually decreases from 10.73 ns (O-GQDs), 9.26 ns (Y-GQDs) to 8.42 ns (G-GQDs) and 4.30 ns for B-GQDs, while the PL decay time of intrinsic state emission (440–560 nm) increases from 2.24 ns (O-GQDs), 1.95 ns (Y-GQDs) to 2.02 ns (G-GQDs) and 3.94 ns for B-GQDs. The varied PL lifetime demonstrates that short emission of B-GQDs mainly depends on the intrinsic state emission (core composition), while the long emissions of O-, Y- and G-GQDs primarily have roots in the defect states emission (surface energy traps) [86,87], which is further confirmed by the aforesaid PL color variation of B-GQDs reduced by NaBH4. The average lifetimes of O-, Y-, G- and B-GQDs respectively are 9.71, 8.27, 7.38 and 4.10 ns, which are obviously longer than these synthetized by the chemical oxidation method (<2.5 ns) [32,49]. From G-GQDs to Y- and O-GQDs, the short lifetime (τ1) alters little, while long lifetime (τ2) gradually enlarges, which implies that the varied lifetime (τavg) may mainly stem from the defect state of GQDs.

Based on the former intrinsic state emission and defect state emission mechanisms, the majority of reports attributed the PL red shift of GQDs to quantum size effects [88,89] and surface defects [49,90]. Chen's groups carried out a series of theoretical calculations and confirmed that the size (the effective sp
2 domain or graphitization) of ideal GQD mainly decides on their emission wavelength [91]. The red shift of the emission wavelength with incremental size is because of the delocalized π electrons moving within the conjugated sp
2 carbon network areas and causing the decrease of band gap. In this work, the average size obviously increases from 3.02 ± 0.45 (G-GQDs) to 4.15 ± 0.54 (Y-GQDs) and 4.61 ± 0.88 nm (O-GQDs) in TEM images (Fig. 2C–F), which corresponds to the enlarged graphitization extent of GQDs based on the Raman and XPS conclusions. Accordingly, the emission wavelength of GQDs equipped with high graphitization degree presents red shift. Additionally, Chen and co-workers also figured the emission wavelength of functionalized GQDs with hydroxyl and/or carboxyl groups [91]. Linking both OH and COOH groups to the GQDs, the emission wavelength of oxidized GQDs obviously occurs red shift. The transmittance of OH and/or COOH groups at ca. 3411 cm−1 in the IR image of Fig. 5A distinctly reduces from G- (65.53%), Y- (52.13%) to O-GQDs (44.54%), which convincingly demonstrates the increasing content of OH and/or COOH groups from G-, Y- to O-GQDs. In addition, the fitting peak area ratio of COOH groups at ca. 288.6 eV in the XPS image of Fig. 6A–D obviously enlarges from G- (8.03%), Y- (10.24%) to O-GQDs (13.81%), which confirms the growing amount of COOH groups from G-, Y- to O-GQDs. The obvious increasing COOH and OH groups of GQDs also give rise to the red shift of emission wavelength, which is coincided with previous report [35,49,79]. On the grounds of the above-mentioned analysis, the red shift from G-, Y- to O-GQDs may be not only contributed from the quantum size effects (the increasing graphitization degree), but also resulted from surface defects (surface functionalization of COOH and OH groups). This result also illustrates that the electrochemical method both exfoliates coke into different sizes of GQDs and brings various degrees of surface defects to carbogenic GQDs.

The rough mechanism of the exfoliation for coal-based GQDs could be inferred from the orders of the appearance of the stripped outcomes (Fig. S1), which was further proved by investigating the variation of maximum emission by altering the H2O content and current density applied. The electrochemical exfoliation coke mainly includes two key steps. As shown in Fig. 8 A, step I can be assigned as the conventional electro-chemical “cut-off” stage [13,92]. According to previous reports, •OH and •O radicals which stemmed from water decomposition (Fig. 8B) can take effect as electrochemical “scissors” to peel off the coke into multilayer graphene sheets by the highly effective electrochemical oxidative cleavage [10,68]. During electrolysis, the active •OH and •O radicals fiercely assault the coke edge planes and vastly accelerate the hydroxylation or oxidation progress of the edge, which significantly facilitates the edge planes to unfold the more edge sheets [93]. The opened edge sheets make it easier for SO4 •− radicals to successfully intercalate into the sheets. Then, the sheets are split and the achieved products swell along the stacking direction, which makes a great contribution to favorably destroy the weaker aliphatic CC bonds. While, the stronger aromatic CC bonds maintain intact in most situations, and the integrated aromatic crystalline carbon structures form nano-scale GQDs [94]. As a consequence, other things being equal, the increasing H2O will be electrolyzed and give rise to larger concentration of •OH and •O radicals in the electrolyte solution. And a mass of radicals forcefully shear the coke into smaller graphene sheets which are further exfoliated into more nano-sized GQDs by SO4 •− free radicals with fluorescence blue shift. In practice, 10% H2O content is enough to come into being sufficient quantities of •OH and •O radicals, and they can attack the coke anode to bring about the corrosion and extension of the coke [15]. In general, the corrosion procedure initially takes place (before the expansion of the coke electrode) at defect sites and boundary sites. Furthermore, the generated hydroxyl and oxygen radicals directly lead to the dissolution of some fluorescent carbon nanometer particles. In brief, a higher H2O content will effectively lead to increased amount of GQDs with fluorescence-emitting blue shift. In contrast, a lower H2O content is beneficial to enlarge content of GQDs with fluorescence-emitting red shift.

To verify the above speculation, we did a series of experiments. With other conditions keeping constant and decreasing the H2O content, the maximum emission peak of the GQDs conducted an apparent red shift (Fig. 9 A). This phenomenon manifests that the maximum emission peak is impacted by the H2O content in some degree, which is in line with our above assumption. Therefore the fluorescent GQDs are precisely regulated by simply controlling the water content in the mixed electrolyte. Additionally, good conductivity of coke significantly facilitates to shear the coke into graphene sheets and form GQDs [4,71].

In step II, (NH4)2S2O8 as particular electrolyte becomes essential for the synthesis of GQDs. As confirmed, the S2O8 2− ions are generated by a high voltage discharging of SO4 2− ions [70]. By the same token, SO4 •−, a kind of strong oxidizing free radicals also formed (Fig. 8A) via the electrochemical reaction of S2O8 2− ions [65]. It's believed that the active SO4 •− radicals can work as another sharp electrochemical “scissors” to powerfully carve up the graphene sheets into nano-dimensions crystalline carbon clusters, namely GQDs by breaking the amorphous aliphatic CC bonds [68,95]. Taking into account the above factors, we may safely reach the conclusion that (NH4)2S2O8 as a specific supporting electrolyte acts as a vital role to form strong SO4 •− free radicals which are crucial towards the generation of GQDs. The electrochemical reaction mechanism involve the complex interactions of the •OH and •O radicals from H2O as well as SO4 •− anions, the equations are proposed as seen in Fig. 8B [15].

Additionally, according to previous literature reports [13,63], current density plays a crucial part in the preparation of GQDs. The higher current density can make a significant contribution to powerfully exfoliate coke particles into smaller size GQDs [61]. Therefore a higher current density will give rise to incremental amount of GQDs with emitting at shorter emission wavelengths or colder fluorescent colors [96]. On the contrary, a lower current density will bring about enhancive number of GQDs with giving out longer emission wavelengths or warmer fluorescent colors. To verify the hypothesis, the current densities were altered with other conditions keeping fixed. As shown in Fig. 9B–C, the GQDs were synthesized at current densities of 240, 160, 120, 60, 30 mA cm−2 (from left to right) and the H2O content of 40%, with the colors of GQDs gradually changing from green to orange for a redshift. In addition, with the water content keeping the same, it can be observed that the maximum emission wave conducts an evident blue shift when the current densities increase from 40, 80, 160–240 mA cm−2 (Fig. 9A). These phenomena are in agreement with previous report [13,63].

To further verify the function of electricity, control experiment of electric current was carried out. The coke was soaked in the electrolyte without applying electricity for 1 h. Whereas it was not observed GQDs in the solutions under the UV light (Fig. S2C), which demonstrated that electric currents played a crucial role in the reaction. In a word, there are two primary factors that influence this reaction system including: the H2O content in electrolyte solution and current density. Consequently, by adjusting the water content and current density, we can accurately regulate the synthesized multi-color GQDs with regular variation in size from 3.02 to 4.61 nm.

ce:section.ce:section-title: The chemical reduction mechanism by NaBH4
ce:section.ce:para: It has been reported in the literature that NaBH4, a strong reductive agent, can reduce CO to OH [73,77,97,98]. Hence, the CO ratio decreased but the content of OH increased after the GQDs were treated with NaBH4, which corresponds to our IR and XPS conclusions [99]. According to literature reports, the short wavelength such as blue emission primarily results from the intrinsic state emission (core composition), while the long wavelength for example green emission mainly derives from the defect states [87]. After introduction of NaBH4, the CO groups on the surface of G-GQDs selectively transformed into OH groups along with decreased surface defects (Fig. 10 ). As an electron donator, the enhanced OH groups not only suppressed non-radiative recombination but also significantly increased the integrity of π conjugated system [78]. In consequence, the intrinsic state emission of reduced GQDs plays the dominant role rather than defect state emission, which resulted in the G-GQDs turning to blue fluorescent GQDs after reducing [58,100].

In summary, we have reported the successful preparation of multi-color GQDs from low-cost coke by an electrochemical method with emission wavelengths from 500 to 560 nm. More importantly, by adjusting H2O content in the electrolyte solution and current density applied to the coke, O, Y and G-GQDs were of regular micro-variation in size from 3.02 to 4.61 nm with high QY of 9.24%, 7.90% and 8.47%, respectively. Additionally, an interesting two-step electrochemical mechanism of GQDs has been fully studied. In short, the developed method owns many virtues of inexpensive and available coke material, mild conditions, simplified operational procedure, short reaction time and high productivities. As far as we know, this is the first article on electrochemical preparation of GQDs from coal. Moreover, the bright B-GQDs are got with a QY as high as 19.27% by NaBH4 reduction. Our work will make the preparation methods of coal-based GQDs more diversified and meaningful. It can also make a great contribution to in-depth developments in coal industries.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This work was financially supported by the National Natural Science Foundation of China (Grant Nos. 21475046, 21427809 and 21645004) and the Fundamental Research Funds for the Central Universities (Grant Nos. 2015ZP028 and 2017MS094).

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para:
The following is the supplementary data related to this article:
Revised Supplementary Revised Supplementary

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data related to this article can be found at https://doi.org/10.1016/j.carbon.2018.08.067.
